sections.header: Verses 1–13 — "Everyone Who Calls on the Name of the Lord"

heb.paragraph: Telos nomou in v. 4 is one of the most debated phrases in Romans. Does it mean Christ is the ‘end’ of the law (its termination) or the ‘goal’ of the law (its fulfilment)? The semantic range of telos supports both: Christ terminates the law as a means of justification while fulfilling the law’s original intention. Paul’s immediate context favours ‘culmination’: Christ is the goal toward which the law always pointed, and his coming renders the law’s regulatory function obsolete for those who believe.

Homologeō in v. 9 (‘if you declare with your mouth, “Jesus is Lord”’) denotes public, binding declaration. In the Roman world, the confession ‘Jesus is Lord’ (Kyrios Iēsous) was a counter-imperial claim: Caesar is not lord. The pairing of mouth-confession and heart-belief (vv. 9–10) unites external profession with internal conviction.

panels.ctx: Romans 10 shifts from divine sovereignty (ch. 9) to human responsibility. Paul’s heart’s desire for Israel is their salvation (v. 1). Their problem is not lack of zeal but misdirected zeal: they sought to establish their own righteousness rather than submit to God’s (v. 3). The confession formula in vv. 9–10 is widely regarded as an early Christian creed, pairing ‘Jesus is Lord’ (the earliest Christian confession, cf. 1 Cor 12:3; Phil 2:11) with belief in the resurrection. The universalism of v. 13 (‘everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved’) is striking in context: Joel 2:32 originally referred to YHWH, and Paul applies it to Jesus, implying the highest possible Christology.

cross.note: Paul reinterprets Moses’ ‘accessibility of the word’ passage: the word of faith is near, not in heaven or across the sea.
Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved — applied by Paul to Jesus Christ.
At the name of Jesus every knee will bow and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord.

notes.note: Israel’s zeal without knowledge is a warning to all religious people. Sincerity without truth leads to self-righteousness rather than God’s righteousness.
Christ is the ‘end’ of the law for righteousness. This does not abolish the law’s moral content but terminates the law as a system for achieving right standing with God.
The twofold condition for salvation: confess with the mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in the heart that God raised him from the dead. Both are necessary; neither alone is sufficient.
There is no distinction between Jew and Gentile: the same Lord is rich toward all who call on him. The universality of the gospel eliminates ethnic privilege.

Calvin reads telos as both ‘end’ and ‘fulfilment.’ Christ fulfils the law’s demands on behalf of believers, and in so doing terminates the law as a covenant of works. The law’s purpose was always to point beyond itself to Christ.
Calvin insists that mouth-confession and heart-belief are inseparable. A faith that remains private is not saving faith. True belief necessarily produces public confession.

The word telos has a range of meanings including ‘goal,’ ‘end,’ ‘fulfilment,’ and ‘culmination.’ The NET translates ‘culmination’ to capture the dual sense: Christ is what the law was aiming at, and his arrival marks the end of the law as a means to righteousness.
Paul’s use of Deuteronomy 30:12–14 is a creative midrashic reinterpretation. The original text spoke of the accessibility of Torah; Paul applies it to the accessibility of Christ and the gospel.

Moo argues that this section balances the sovereignty emphasis of chapter 9 with the universal offer of the gospel. God’s sovereign election does not eliminate the need for preaching, believing, and confessing. The human response is genuine even within the framework of divine sovereignty.
Moo takes telos as ‘culmination’ rather than simple ‘termination.’ Christ is the goal the law was always running toward, and his coming transforms the law’s role in the life of God’s people.

Schreiner argues that Israel’s guilt lies in the refusal to submit to God’s righteousness. They were not ignorant of God’s standards but chose to pursue their own. The fault is moral, not merely intellectual.
Schreiner emphasises the Christological import of v. 13: Paul applies Joel’s YHWH text to Jesus, implying that calling on Jesus is calling on God. This is implicit affirmation of Christ’s deity.

heb.gloss: to proclaim / to preach / to herald
heb.paragraph: Kēryssō in vv. 14–15 denotes authoritative proclamation, as a herald announces the decree of a king. Paul’s chain of logic runs backward: calling presupposes believing, believing presupposes hearing, hearing presupposes preaching, preaching presupposes sending. The entire chain depends on God’s initiative in commissioning messengers, making mission the outworking of sovereign mercy.

panels.ctx: Romans 10:14–21 presents Paul’s famous ‘chain of salvation’: sending → preaching → hearing → believing → calling. The citation of Isaiah 52:7 (‘how beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news’) connects the gospel mission to the Isaianic herald announcing God’s reign. Paul’s argument then turns to Israel’s failure: they heard but did not believe (vv. 16–21). The chapter ends with Moses and Isaiah testifying that God stretched out his hands to a disobedient and obstinate people. Israel’s rejection is not because the message was unclear but because they refused to obey it.

cross.note: The herald announcing peace and good news — applied by Paul to gospel messengers.
Who has believed our message? — Paul uses this to explain Israel’s unbelief.
Moses predicted that God would provoke Israel to jealousy through a non-nation — the Gentile mission.

notes.note: The missionary chain is inviolable: no one can believe without hearing, and no one can hear without a preacher. This passage is the biblical foundation for the urgency of world missions.
Faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ. The instrumentality of the preached word is non-negotiable: God works through proclamation.
Israel’s excuse is not ignorance but obstinacy. They heard the message (v. 18) and understood it (v. 19) but chose not to obey. Paul quotes Moses, Isaiah, and the Psalms to prove the point.

Calvin draws out the implication: if preaching is necessary for faith, then the church’s obligation to send preachers is urgent and non-negotiable. The beautiful feet are those of every faithful minister of the gospel.

The pathos of God stretching out his hands to a disobedient people reveals divine patience. Calvin sees this as the outward call that Israel rejected, contrasted with the effectual call given to the elect.

The Isaiah 52:7 quotation originally described the herald announcing Israel’s return from Babylonian exile. Paul applies it typologically to the gospel proclamation of God’s eschatological saving act.
Paul quotes Psalm 19:4 (‘their voice has gone out into all the earth’) about creation’s testimony to argue that the gospel has been sufficiently proclaimed. The application extends the psalm’s scope.

Moo reads this section as Paul’s argument that Israel’s rejection is culpable, not excusable. They heard the message, understood its implications, and chose disobedience. The missionary chain demonstrates that God has done everything necessary; the failure lies with the hearers.

Schreiner emphasises that the chain of vv. 14–15 makes human responsibility real within the framework of divine sovereignty. God sovereignly elects (ch. 9) and sovereignly sends messengers (10:15), but human beings genuinely hear, believe, and call.
Schreiner argues that Israel’s failure is both predicted by Scripture and culpable in execution. Divine foreknowledge of Israel’s disobedience does not excuse it.

themes.note: Faith peaks at 10: the chapter defines faith’s content (confession and belief) and means (hearing the preached word). Mission at 9 reflects the missionary chain that is foundational to evangelism.

rows.label: vv.5–13
rows.text: The Word Is Near: Confess and Believe

rows.label: vv.14–17
rows.text: The Missionary Chain: Sending, Preaching, Hearing, Believing

lit.note: Romans 10 shifts from God’s sovereignty (ch. 9) to human responsibility. The universal offer of salvation (v. 13) and the missionary mandate (vv. 14–15) exist alongside Israel’s culpable rejection (vv. 18–21).

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Moses
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Word of Faith

translations.text: Christ is the culmination of the law so that there may be righteousness for everyone who believes.
For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes.
Brothers and sisters, my heart’s desire and prayer to God for the Israelites is that they may be saved.
Brothers, my heart’s desire and prayer to God for them is that they may be saved.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Word of Faith within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
